coredata.dc:title: CTAB assisted hydrothermal synthesis of 0D / 2D structure carbon quantum dots BiOIO3 composite for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xu
Jing, Hongxia
Gao, Yanlin
Xin, Yongzhi
Li, Qiaoling
coredata.dc:description: Semiconductor photocatalysts have broad application prospects in the degradation of antibiotics and other pollutants. In this paper, the composite of carbon quantum dot modified BiOIO3 (CQDs / BiOIO3) was prepared by CTAB assisted one-step hydrothermal method. The samples were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, TEM, SEM and so on, it was found that the two-dimensional sheet structure of BiOIO3 was loaded by carbon quantum dot, which form a 0D / 2D structure of CQDs / BiOIO3. The results showed that the CQDs / BiOIO3 composite can degrade 70% tetracycline aqueous solution within 120 min, which is greatly enhanced compared with pure BiOIO3 (50% within 120 min). Through the active species trapping experiment, it was explored that ·O2¯ was the main active substance, and the possible mechanism was further proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs / BiOIO3
0D / 2D structure
Surfactant
Catalytic mechanism
Tetracycline

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of experimental preparation process.
Morphology of BiOIO3-0 (a); BiOIO3-2 (b) and BC-10 (c-e).
FT-IR spectra of BiOIO3-0, BiOIO3-2 and BC-10.
UV–vis DRS (a) and PL emission spectra (b) of CQDs.
UV–vis DRS (a) and the band gaps (b) of different materials; M−S curves (c) of BC-10.
PL emission spectra (a) and Impedance diagrams (b) of different materials.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm (a), and pore size distribution (b) of BiOIO3-0 and BiOIO3-2.
Degradation curves (a-c), Kinetic curves (d) and TOC removal efficiency (e) of different materials under simulated sunlight irradiation.
Histogram of active substance detection in BC-10.
The possible photocatalytic mechanism of BC-10.

head.ce:title: CTAB assisted hydrothermal synthesis of 0D / 2D structure carbon quantum dots BiOIO3 composite for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: JinXi Industries Group Co., LTD, Taiyuan 030027, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: JinXi Industries Group Co., LTD, Taiyuan 030027, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the composite of carbon quantum dot modified BiOIO3 (CQDs / BiOIO3) was prepared by CTAB assisted one step hydrothermal method. The results showed that the CQDs / BiOIO3 composite can degrade 70% tetracycline aqueous solution within 120 min. And the possible mechanism was further proposed.

Semiconductor photocatalysts have broad application prospects in the degradation of antibiotics and other pollutants. In this paper, the composite of carbon quantum dot modified BiOIO3 (CQDs / BiOIO3) was prepared by CTAB assisted one-step hydrothermal method. The samples were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, TEM, SEM and so on, it was found that the two-dimensional sheet structure of BiOIO3 was loaded by carbon quantum dot, which form a 0D / 2D structure of CQDs / BiOIO3. The results showed that the CQDs / BiOIO3 composite can degrade 70% tetracycline aqueous solution within 120 min, which is greatly enhanced compared with pure BiOIO3 (50% within 120 min). Through the active species trapping experiment, it was explored that ·O2¯ was the main active substance, and the possible mechanism was further proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are mainly secondary metabolites produced by bacteria, fungi, actinomycetes and other microorganisms in the process of life. In addition, some medicinal antibiotics are excessively discharged into the environment through feces, urine and other means after human use. Due to the low efficiency of natural degradation, they caused persistent pollution to the environment [1,2]. Tetracycline, one of the representative drugs of antibiotics, is widely used all over the world, including additives for human and animal treatment of infectious diseases, animal feed, aquaculture, fungal growth inhibitors of fruit trees and so on [3]. In order to improve the removal rate of tetracycline antibiotics in aqueous system, a series of methods and strategies have been designed, including adsorption [4], ion exchange [5] and photocatalysis [6,7]. Among them, photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline is one of the most promising technologies. In particular, semiconductor photocatalysts use clean and renewable solar energy to degrade organic pollutants, which has been proved to be a “green” way, especially to solve the two key factors threatening: sustainable development, environmental pollution and energy crisis [8,9].

At present, the intensity of environmental governance is gradually increasing. Photocatalytic technology presents a good development prospect because of its clean and efficient characteristics. Since the application of TiO2 materials in the field of photocatalysis in 1972, various semiconductor photocatalytic materials came into being [10]. In recent years, BiOX (X = Cl, I, Br) have good chemical stability and unique electronic band structure under visible light irradiation, and have shown excellent photocatalytic performance. Most of them are layered compounds composed of [Bi2O2]2+ and double-layer halogen elements [11], for example, BiOI, BiOBr and BiOCl etc. [12–15]. As a new bismuth based material, BiOIO3 has the highest VB value (about 3.2 V vs NHE) in the whole bismuth based semiconductor material [16]. Structurally, the self-polarized electric field is generated due to its unique crystal structure (Bi2O2)2+ and IO3¯, which has great development potential [17,18]. At the same time, BiOIO3 has insufficient utilization of visible light due to its wide band gap, which limits its practical application. Therefore, in order to improve the utilization range of solar energy spectrum, researchers have tried different methods and strategies to synthesize BiOIO3. For example, composites with significantly increased degradation capacity can be obtained by establishing CSs-BiOI/BiOIO3 heterostructures [19], building oxygen vacancy [20], synthesis of N-BiOIO3 by simple nitrogen doping [21]. However, these preparation processes still have the disadvantages of complex operation and strict conditions.

As a kind of popular carbon material lately, carbon quantum dots were accidentally made in 2004 [22]. Since then, it has been widely concerned by scientists because of its excellent optical properties, and has been applied in biomedicine, fluorescent probes, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis and other fields [23–26]. CQDs are centered on sp2 carbon clusters and surrounded by a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. This special structure can effectively store electrons and provide an ideal place for the migration of photogenerated charges. In addition, CQDs have been proved to induce different photoluminescence bands, which is due to the local electronic states formed by their surface functional groups [27]. Therefore, carbon quantum dots can improve the degradation efficiency in the process of photocatalysis in two aspects. First, the upconversion luminescence behavior (UCPL) of carbon quantum dots is used to convert the long wavelength excitation source into short wavelength light, so as to improve the utilization of visible light [28]. Second, the strong electrophilic behavior of carbon quantum dots can guide electrons to shuttle freely, then improve the mobility of photogenerated electrons [29].

In this work, we designed and synthesized 0D / 2D CQDs/BiOIO3 composite structurally, which exhibited high utilization rate of sunlight spectrum and excellent photocatalytic efficiency. In order to realize the interaction between the electronic mediator of CQDs and the self-polarized electric field of BiOIO3, the 0D / 2D CQDs/BiOIO3 composite was prepared by simple one-step hydrothermal method. In the preparation process of ultra-thin flake BiOIO3, the surfactant was used to alter the distribution of pore structure to improve the photocatalytic efficiency, which was another innovation of this work. Eventually, tetracycline aqueous solution was degraded under simulated sunlight and a possible catalytic mechanism was proposed accordingly.

Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium iodate (KIO3) and cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Industrial Corporation. All reagents were analytically pure and did not need further purification.

CQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. A certain amount of citric acid was added to 25 mL of deionized water and 268
μ L
diethylamine was added after uniform mixing. Then the composite was transferred to the 35 mL reactor and heated it at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, it was dialyzed in a 1 kD cut-off flow dialysis bag for 48 h to obtain CQDs aqueous solution.

Firstly, 1.25 mmol, 1.5 mmol and 1.75 mmol of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O were added to 15 mL of deionized water respectively. After mixing evenly, KIO3 with the same molar ratio was added under ultrasonic conditions, and then 0.05 g of cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was added while stirring. Then, the suspension was transferred to the 35 mL reactor and reacted at 120 ℃ for 12 h to obtain the target product. The mass ratio of CTAB and BiOIO3 were 1:17, 1:20, 1:24, which were named as BiOIO3-1、BiOIO3-2 and BiOIO3-3 respectively. For comparison, the BiOIO3 without CTAB was prepared and named as BiOIO3-0.

In this experiment, CQDs/BiOIO3 was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Different volumes of CQDs aqueous solutions (5 mL, 10 mL and 15 mL) were added to the BiOIO3 material with the best proportion. After stirring for 30 min, it continued to be ultrasonic for 30 min, and then transferred it to the 35 mL reactor and reacted at 120 ℃ for 12 h. Then took it out after fully reaction, and washed it with deionized water and absolute ethanol for 3 ∼ 5 times, finally dried it at 80 ℃ to obtain the target products. The calculated mass of carbon quantum dots after freeze-drying shows that the mass ratio of CQDs to BiOIO3 were 1:430, 1:215 and 1:143, which were named as BC-5, BC-10 and BC-15 respectively. The schematic diagram of the experimental process is shown in Fig. 1 .

A 250 W metal halide lamp was used to simulate sunlight irradiation, and the catalytic performance of the material was explored through the photodegradation of tetracycline aqueous solution. Firstly, 2.5 mg photocatalyst was added into 100 mL tetracycline aqueous solution and stirred in dark for 1 h until complete adsorption. Then the above solution was moved to the photoreactor for reaction for 2 h. During the process, the samples were taken every 20 min, and the supernatant was separated from the samples in a centrifuge. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. Finally, the photodegradation efficiency was calculated according to the absorbance.
(1)
η =
C
C 0
× 100 % =
A
A 0
× 100 %

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were performed on a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiated. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were carried out under Thermo Scientific Apreo-2C and Talos F200S respectively. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded on Hitachi F-2700. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) was detected by Micromeritics ASAP 2460. Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured by TOC analyzer (Shimadzu TOC–L). The electrochemical impedance spectra and M−S spectra of the samples were measured on bio logic (vsp-300) using three-electrode cell. The samples were dissolved in ethanol and coated on the surface of nickel foam as working electrodes. The platinum and saturated calomel electrodes were used as counter electrode and reference electrode respectively.

According to the characterization results of XRD spectra (Fig. 2 ), it can be noticed that the diffraction peaks of BiOIO3 (ICSD No. 26–2019) appear at 27.46°, 31.68°, 32.20°and 32.46°, corresponding to (121), (002), (200) and (040) crystal planes respectively [30]. Besides, due to the amorphous state of CQDs, the diffraction peak of CQDs cannot be observed in the XRD pattern of BC-10 after loading CQDs. Notably, the diffraction peak of BC-10 at 27.46° is significantly weakened and widened due to the recombination of CQDs, indicating that the low crystallinity of CQDs may inhibit the crystal growth of BiOIO3.

As presented in Fig. 3 , the morphology of BiOIO3-0、BiOIO3-2 and BC-10 are explored by SEM, TEM and HRTEM respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 3a that BiOIO3-0 display a sheet structure with smooth surface typically, and Fig. 3b is the SEM of BiOIO3-2, which presented a rough state due to the addition of CTAB. Moreover, Fig. 3c-e is the TEM diagram of BC-10 with different scales. From Fig. 3c, we can see the obvious shadow part, where the color is deepened due to the load of CQDs. In Fig. 3d, the existence of carbon dots can be clearly observed, the diameter was about 1–3 nm, indicating that the CQDs / BiOIO3 compound with 0D / 2D structure was successfully prepared. And after analysis and calculation, the lattice spacing of BiOIO3 is 0.279 nm (Fig. 3e), which is consistent with the (040) plane of BiOIO3 in XRD analysis [31]. These indicated that the (040) crystal plane of BiOIO3 can be effectively exposed when CTAB was added.

According to FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4 ), the peak at 510 cm¯1 is considered to the vibration of Bi − O bond, and the strong peak at 660 cm¯1 and 767 cm¯1 is caused by the stretching vibration of I − O bond, which belong to the characteristic peak of BiOIO3 exactly. It proved that the target compound BiOIO3 has been successfully synthesized. After the introduction of CTAB, both BiOIO3-2 and BC-10 have characteristic peaks of − CH3 (2920 cm¯1, 2850 cm¯1) and − CH2− (1471 cm¯1, 1344 cm¯1), which proved that CTAB has been embedded in BiOIO3. Compared with BiOIO3-0, the two weak peaks of BC-10 at 3448 cm¯1 and 1652 cm¯1 are caused by the tensile vibration of O H bond and the vibration of CO, which proved that CQDs has been successfully loaded on BiOIO3. Besides, it should be noticed that the characteristic peaks were weak due to the small load of CQDs.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 5 a. The peak is concentrated at about 344 nm, which is formed by n-
π
∗
electron transition with oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs. The insertions in Fig. 5a are photos of CQDs under visible light and ultraviolet light respectively, which also confirmed the good optical properties of CQDs. It can be seen from the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 5b) that CQDs has excellent up-conversion luminescence performance (UCPL). Under the excitation wavelength of 600 nm ∼ 800 nm, the up-conversion emission wavelength range is 400 nm ∼ 580 nm. Furthermore, the quantum yield of CQDs can reach about 59% under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the known quinine sulfate as the reference according to the following formula:
(2)
Q
CQDs
=
Q R
×
F
CQDs
F R
×
A R
A
CQDs
×
η
CQDs
2
η
R
2

According to the analysis of UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 6 a), the absorption band of BiOIO3-0 is about 380 nm, indicating that BiOIO3 is mainly activated by ultraviolet light. When CTAB is added, it can be seen that the absorption band of BiOIO3-2 widens to the visible region. It is probably because that CTA+ in Cationic Surfactant CTAB is combined with the compound in precursor through electrostatic force to adsorb on the surface of BiOIO3, resulting in quantum confinement effort. Combined with CQDs, the absorption band of BC-10 can be widened to about 500 nm, which proved that the existence of carbon quantum dots can effectively increase the utilization of visible light in the composite.

As shown in Fig. 6b, the energy band values of BiOIO3-0 was calculated by Eq. 3. Considering BiOIO3 is an indirect semiconductors [32], so n = 4. Therefore, it was obtained that the band gap value of BiOIO3-0 was 3.11 eV.

αhν = A(hν - Eg) n
/2 (3).

In addition, the flat band point voltage (Efb) of BiOIO3-0 was calculated by Mott-Schottky method. As shown in Fig. 6c, the slope of M−S curve of BiOIO3-0 is positive, indicating that BiOIO3 belongs to n-type semiconductor structure. And the Efb of BiOIO3-0 is −0.82 eV (vs Ag/AgCl), corresponding to 0.62 eV (vs NHE), which is based on the intercept between the curve abscissa [33]. The ECB of semiconductors is closely related to the Efb. For most n-type semiconductors, the Efb is about 0.1 eV positive than ECB, so the ECB of BiOIO3-0 is −0.72 eV [34]. According to the following formula, the EVB of BiOIO3-0 is 2.39 eV.
(4)
E g
=
E
VB
-
E
CB

The recombination rate of h+-e¯ was studied by fluorescence measurement. As shown in Fig. 7 a, BiOIO3-0 shows a strong fluorescence peak near 400 nm, while the peaks of BiOIO3-2 and BC-10 decrease significantly at 400 nm. It indicated that h+-e¯ could achieve more effective migration, while reduced the recombination rate and enhanced the photocatalytic performance after the introduction of CTAB and CQDs.

In order to further analyze the electronic properties of the photocatalyst, the EIS experiment was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 7b, the semicircle diameter of BC-10 composite is the smallest, indicating that the migration resistance of charge pair of BC-10 is the smallest. It was further confirmed that the introduction of CTAB and CQDs can promote the migration of h+-e¯ and improve the degradation rate of the photocatalyst effectively.

In the Table. 1 , the specific surface area of BiOIO3-0 and BiOIO3-2 are 13.3645 and 12.3883 m2/g respectively, which have little effect on the photocatalytic activity [35]. Nevertheless, the average pore diameter of BiOIO3-2 (15.1159 nm) is much bigger than that of BiOIO3-0 (13.6465 nm), which enhanced the diffusion rate of tetracycline in the catalyst. Thus the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the BiOIO3-2 is greatly improved than that of BiOIO3-0, which is consistent with the experimental results of photodegradation.

The photocatalytic performance of the composites was explored through the degradation of tetracycline. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, after exploring the different reaction temperature and time, the optimal reaction condition was determined as 120℃ for 12 h. Fig. 9c shows the degradation curves of different materials under simulate sunlight. It can be seen that an appropriate amount of CTAB is beneficial to improve the degradation rate of tetracycline relative to BiOIO3. When the mass ratio of CTAB and BiOIO3 is 1:20 (that is BiOIO3-2), the degradation rate is the highest, which can reach 58%. Additionally, when BiOIO3-2 is combined with CQDs, the overall performance is improved. The best degradation result is that when the mass ratio of CQDs to BiOIO3-2 is 1:215 (BC-10), it can degrade 70% of tetracycline aqueous solution within 120 min explicitly.

Fig. 9d shows the degradation dynamics of tetracycline aqueous solution by three materials under simulated sunlight, which conform to the first-order kinetics characteristics. Accordingly, the rate constant k and linear correlation of the materials can be obtained, and the results are shown in the Table. 2 . It can be found that the kinetic constant k of BC-10 (9.37 × 10¯3 min¯1) is 1.27 times and 1.69 times higher than that of BiOIO3-0 (5.68 × 10¯3 min¯1) and BiOIO3-2 (7.35 × 10¯3 min¯1) respectively. Therefore, it is concluded that the CQDs / BiOIO3 composite has excellent photocatalytic performance under simulated sunlight.

Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured to explore the mineralization of tetracycline, and the results are shown in Fig. 9e. After comparing the experimental results, it is found that although the degradation rate of TC can reach 70% within 120 min, the remove rate of TOC can reach 18% at the same time. The difference between them may be due to the other organic intermediate produced after photocatalytic degradation of TC [36].

The active substances in the degradation of tetracycline aqueous solution by BC-10 composite were studied. As shown in Fig. 10 , benzoquinone (BQ), Ethyl- enediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were used as capture agents for ·O2¯, h+ and ·OH separately. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 10, it can be noticed that the degradation rate of tetracycline aqueous solution is the lowed while adding BQ, followed by adding EDTA-2Na. It illustrated that the ·O2¯ was the main active substance, while the effects of ·OH and h+ cannot be ignored.

Based on the above experimental results, the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of BC-10 was proposed in Fig. 11 . The band gap of pure BiOIO3 is 3.11 eV and can be activated under ultraviolet light exclusively. After compounding CTAB and CQDs, the absorption range of BC-10 is effectively extended to the visible region, which can be confirmed in the optical analysis (Fig. 6). Specifically, the existence of surfactants provides an appropriate distribution of the pore structure for the photogenerated electron reaction, the UCPL behavior of CQDs expands the utilization of simulated sunlight and further excites the photogenerated charge of BC-10. Therefore, under the excitation of simulated sunlight, the electrons in the valence band of BiOIO3 can easily jump to the conduction band, and the h+ was left on the valence band. In addition, CQDs can also transmit electrons because of its strong electrophilicity, so the e¯ in the conduction band of BiOIO3 can be transferred to CQDs, thus greatly improving the separation rate of the photogenerated carrier, which can be confirmed by EIS and PL spectra results [30]. According to the M−S results, the EVB value of BiOIO3 is 2.39 eV, which is lower than the potential value of ·OH/OH¯ (1.99 eV) theoretically. Thus, the OH¯ can be oxidized to ·OH, which is also consistent with the experimental results of active substance capture. Moreover, the ECB of BiOIO3 is −0.72 eV, indicating that the excited photogenerated e¯ can reduce O2 to ·O2¯ (0.33 eV). Therefore, as the main active species and auxiliary, ·O2¯ and ·OH respectively oxidize TC to CO2, H2O and other intermediates, which is consistent with the experiment results of active species capture [2]. To sum up, the possible photo- degradation pathways were proposed conclusively as follows:

BC-10
+ h ν → e Â ¯ +
h
+
e Â ¯ +
O 2
→ Â · O 2 Â ¯
h
+
+ O H Â ¯ → Â · O H
O 2 Â ¯ / Â · O H + T C → I n t e r m e d i a t e s +
CO
2
+
H 2
O

In this paper, CQDs/BiOIO3 composites were successfully prepared by CTAB assisted one-step hydrothermal method. Compared with other existing methods, we synthesized photocatalytic composites with excellent performance through a simpler synthesis method. By means of morphological characterization, optical properties and structural analysis proved that BC-10 composites have better photocatalytic performance than pure BiOIO3. Under the condition of simulated sunlight irradiation, BC-10 composite can reduce tetracycline by about 70% in 120 min, and the rate constant was 9.37 × 10¯3. Finally, the mechanism study shows that ·O2¯ was the main active substance in the whole degradation process, and the possible degradation pathways were proposed. This experiment provided an innovative idea for improving environmental pollution and energy issues.

Xu Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Hongxia Jing: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Yanlin Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yongzhi Xin: Formal analysis, Resources. Qiaoling Li: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
